The council voted to restrict evening gatherings after the incident. Not everyone agrees it was necessary.
A volunteer rotation for the night watch fell apart because the same three families kept getting skipped. Now nobody volunteers at all.
Two families that used to share a well are now drawing water at different hours to avoid each other.
The new arrivals were given plots on the eastern edge. Some of the established residents think that is too generous. Others say it is not generous enough.
A petition went around last week asking for the night curfew to be lifted. It got enough signatures to force a discussion.
The healer left three months ago and nobody has stepped up to replace them. Minor injuries are becoming serious ones.
People trust the watch captain, but there is quiet disagreement about who he answers to.
The trade committee doubled the stall fees without warning. Half the vendors threatened to leave. Two actually did.
There used to be a shared meal every rest day. It stopped when someone accused the organizers of favoritism in the seating.
The population has grown faster than the housing. Three families are still doubled up in temporary shelters.
A group of residents started patrolling the perimeter on their own. The official guard sees it as either helpful or insulting depending on who you ask.
The elder who used to mediate disputes passed away last winter. Nobody has the same authority and it shows.
Newcomers are expected to contribute labor for the first season. It is not written anywhere but everyone knows.
The argument over water rights has been going on longer than some residents have been alive.
Someone proposed rotating the council seats every season. The current members were not enthusiastic.
Children from different parts of the settlement do not play together the way they used to. They have picked up their parents' grudges.
A public apology was offered and rejected. Now both sides feel wronged and the rift is wider than before.
The blacksmith and the carpenter had a falling out over shared wall space. Neither will move their workshop and now the apprentices relay messages between them.
A family left in the middle of the night without telling anyone. Their plot is still empty and nobody wants to claim it because of what people might think.
The grain allocation favors the founding families by a ratio that has never been formally justified. The newer residents have noticed.
A dispute over fence lines turned into a dispute over the original survey markers. The markers were placed by someone no longer living, so there is no authority to settle it.
The midwife is training an apprentice, which is overdue. She is the only one who knows the work and she has been doing it for thirty years without a break.
The council minutes are supposed to be public but they are kept in the records hall, which is locked outside of council hours. Convenient for the council.
A merchant who was caught short-weighing was fined but allowed to continue trading. The customers he cheated think the fine was too light. The merchant thinks it was too harsh. Nobody is satisfied.

The road crew stopped maintaining the eastern approach because they say the council is not paying them enough. The council says the road crew is overstating the work required. The road gets worse while they argue.
Two of the council members are related by marriage. Nobody brought it up when they were both appointed but now that they vote together on everything, people are talking.

The herbalist set up a garden on common ground without asking. When confronted, she pointed out that nobody else was using it and that her remedies benefit everyone. She has a point, but the precedent worries people.
The tavern owner waters down the ale and everyone knows it. He claims the supply is short. The brewer, who delivers the supply, disagrees publicly.

A woman found a cache of old documents in the foundation of her home during repairs. They appear to be original settlement charters. The council wants to examine them. She wants to keep them. The law is unclear.
The fire watch rotation has gaps because there are not enough volunteers. The last time a fire started at night, it burned for twenty minutes before anyone responded.

A young couple wants to build a home on an unoccupied lot near the gate. The council says the lot is reserved for future defense structures. The couple says the defense plans have been future for eight years.
The schoolmaster resigned after a disagreement with three families about what should be taught. The children have been without lessons for two months.
Someone has been leaving anonymous notes on the council board criticizing specific decisions. The handwriting is distinctive but nobody will identify the author publicly.
The communal oven broke down and the repair has been delayed because the mason and the council cannot agree on who pays. Meanwhile, twelve households have no way to bake.
A merchant caravan bypassed us entirely last season because the road was in such poor condition. The economic loss was felt by every vendor in the market.

The well in the upper quarter produces better water than the one in the lower quarter. Residents in the lower quarter want access to the upper well. Residents in the upper quarter say it cannot handle the additional demand.

The founding charter says all residents have equal voice in council matters. In practice, the original families speak more and are heard more. Nobody enforces the charter because the original families wrote it.

The tanner was asked to relocate his operation downwind because of the smell. He refused on the grounds that his location predates most of the complainants' homes. Both positions are technically valid.
A memorial was erected for the settlers lost in the first winter. The names are worn and some residents want it restored. Others say the funds should go to the living.
The night patrol reported hearing raised voices from the council hall well after the scheduled meeting time. Whatever was discussed, it did not make the official minutes.
A group of younger residents have been advocating for an elected council instead of the appointed one. The current council says elections would be divisive. The advocates say that is the point.
The shared granary operates on an honor system for contributions. Three families have been contributing less than their share and everyone knows which three.

A trade agreement with a neighboring settlement fell through because our council could not agree on terms in time. The other settlement signed with someone else. An embarrassment that could have been avoided.

The woodcutter's guild set minimum prices and the independent cutters are furious. They say the guild is eliminating competition. The guild says it is protecting quality standards. Both are probably right.
A stray dog adopted by the children has become a point of contention. Some residents feed it. Others say it is a pest. The council was actually asked to rule on the status of a dog.

The old meeting hall has termite damage in the support beams. An inspection confirmed it months ago but the repair estimate exceeds the discretionary fund. The council is debating whether to repair, rebuild, or relocate. In the meantime, people are meeting in a building that might collapse.

A farmer diverted part of the irrigation channel to his own fields, reducing flow to three downstream neighbors. He says the water was being wasted. They say it was being used. The resolution is pending and the crops are not waiting.
The guard captain's son was given a post without the usual probationary period. Nobody has complained officially, but the other recruits who served their full probation are not quiet about it.

A widow with four children has been receiving extra rations from the storehouse. Some residents support this. Others say the policy should apply equally to all single-parent households, of which there are seven.
The messenger service between our settlement and the next has become unreliable. Two dispatches went unanswered last month. Whether the problem is on our end or theirs is itself a matter of dispute.

A public well was contaminated after someone dumped waste too close to the water table. The culprit was identified but denied responsibility. The evidence is circumstantial and the council does not want to act without proof.

The bakery and the mill have an exclusive arrangement that prevents other bakers from buying grain at wholesale. It is technically legal but it has doubled the cost of bread for anyone outside the arrangement.

The council banned open fires within fifty paces of the granary after a scare. The blacksmith, whose forge is within that radius, was given an exemption. The potter, whose kiln is also within range, was not. The inconsistency has been noted.

A proposal to extend the wall to include the eastern homesteads was rejected on cost grounds. The families living outside the wall feel abandoned. The council says the math does not work. The families say their safety is not a math problem.
The water carrier has been overcharging and nobody has challenged it because he is the only one willing to do the job. A monopoly of one is still a monopoly.
Two elders have not spoken to each other in four years. Nobody remembers the original cause but both have accumulated allies and the division runs through every public decision.

A young apprentice was dismissed from the workshop for insubordination. She claims she was dismissed for pointing out a safety hazard. The workshop master says the two events are unrelated. The timing suggests otherwise.

The tax collector takes his percentage before the goods reach the market. Vendors say this means they are taxed on potential sales, not actual ones. The collector says he is following the schedule the council approved.
A bridge repair was funded by voluntary contributions. The three wealthiest families contributed nothing and used the bridge the most. The resentment has not faded.
The latrine pits need to be moved further from the creek. Everyone agrees on this. Nobody agrees on where they should go instead because every proposed location is near someone's home.
The courier was caught reading sealed messages. He was reprimanded but not dismissed because there is no replacement available. Trust in the courier system has understandably declined.

A celebration was organized to mark the settlement's anniversary. Attendance was lower than expected because the organizing committee excluded three families from the planning. The families say the exclusion was deliberate. The committee says it was an oversight.
The shared tool shed has been locked since someone stole a saw. Everyone is now inconvenienced because of one thief, and the council's solution punishes the honest majority.

A widow donated her late husband's land to the settlement for common use. The council accepted the donation but then leased the land to a specific family. The widow is upset because that was not the intent.

The seamstress raised her prices by a third and half her clients went to the next settlement for alterations. She says the material costs justify it. The clients say she is gouging. The material costs did go up, but not by a third.

The night soil collectors have threatened to stop working unless their pay is increased. The council says the budget cannot accommodate it. The collectors pointed out that the alternative is disease. The council found the budget.

A hunter was accused of taking game from another family's trap line. The boundaries of trap lines are customary, not surveyed. The accused says he was within his rights. The accuser says everyone knows where the line is.

The council appointed a record-keeper who cannot read well. She was chosen because she is the council chair's cousin. The previous record-keeper, who was literate and competent, was not offered the position.

A merchant proposed building a permanent covered market. The council demanded a percentage of stall revenue in exchange for the land. The merchant says the percentage is too high. The council says the land is valuable. The market remains uncovered.
The watch rotation puts the newest recruits on the most dangerous shift. The senior guards say it builds character. The recruits say it disproportionately risks their lives. Both are correct.

A shared pasture is being overgrazed because there is no limit on how many animals each household can put on it. Proposing a limit means telling specific families to reduce their herds. Nobody wants to be the one to say it.

A building that was supposed to be a schoolhouse was repurposed as additional council offices. The families with children were told the schoolhouse was a long-term plan. They feel the plan was abandoned without consultation.

The road to the trading post was widened last year at considerable cost. The primary beneficiaries were two merchant families who lobbied for it. The labor was drawn from the general pool. The rest of the settlement paid in sweat for someone else's profit.

An informal court of opinion has formed around the tavern hearth. Grievances are aired, judgments are rendered by whoever speaks loudest, and the council pretends it does not happen. It is not justice, but it is faster than the official process.

The well digger found a second aquifer at forty feet and wants to develop it as a private water source. The council says subsurface water belongs to the settlement. The well digger says the law does not address subsurface resources because it was written before anyone dug that deep.
A charitable fund established for emergencies has been drawn on three times this year for non-emergencies. The contributors are questioning whether their donations are being used as intended.

The gravedigger is the most avoided person in the settlement despite being one of the most essential. He eats alone, drinks alone, and the council pays him less than the road crew. Someone ought to correct that.

A formal complaint was filed against the council for failing to publish the annual budget. The council says the budget is available on request. The complainant says he requested it and was told to come back later. He came back later and was told it was under revision.

The potter and the brickmaker both claim the right to the clay deposit north of the settlement. The deposit was discovered by neither of them: a child found it while playing. The child has received no consideration.

A delegation from a neighboring settlement visited to discuss trade terms and was received poorly. The housing they were given was substandard and the council meeting was delayed twice. Whether this was incompetence or deliberate disrespect is a matter of local debate.

The healer charges based on ability to pay, which means some people pay nothing. Those who pay full price resent subsidizing their neighbors. The healer says it is the only ethical model. The paying patients say ethics are easier when someone else foots the bill.
A boundary wall between two properties was built by one party and the other refuses to pay their share. The builder says custom demands equal contribution. The neighbor says custom is not law.
The grain store has a rat problem that the storehouse keeper attributes to poor construction. The builder attributes it to poor maintenance. The rats, unbothered by the debate, continue eating.

A retired guard was denied his pension because the council says the pension fund was never formally established. The guard says he was promised a pension when he enlisted. No written record exists of the promise.

The cobbler makes excellent boots and terrible sandals. Everyone knows this but people keep ordering sandals from him because he is closer than the next cobbler. He keeps making them because people keep buying them. The market is not always rational.

A communal woodpile was established near the gate for emergency fuel. It has been steadily shrinking despite no emergencies. Someone is taking from it, and the honor system that was supposed to prevent this is not working.

The council chair has served for nine consecutive terms. There is no term limit because nobody anticipated someone would want the job for that long. Removing him would require a charter amendment, which he would have to approve.

A young farmer's crop was trampled by livestock that broke through a shared fence. The livestock owner says the fence was the farmer's responsibility. The farmer says the livestock owner's animals are known escape artists. The fence quality is debatable. The crop loss is not.

The apprenticeship system pairs young workers with masters for three years. Two apprentices have complained that their masters use them as unpaid labor rather than teaching them the trade. The guild says it will investigate. The investigation has been pending for five months.

A woman who lost her home to a fire was told she could not rebuild in the same location because the council has reclassified the area. She says the reclassification happened after the fire and suspects the council wanted the land.

The burial ground is nearly full and the council has been avoiding the question of where the next one will be. Every proposed site is near someone's home or farm. The dead need somewhere to go and the living keep saying not here.

The tax assessment was raised for the third consecutive year but the services funded by the tax have not improved. The roads are worse, the watch is understaffed, and the communal buildings need repair. The council says the costs have gone up. Nobody disputes that. They dispute the priorities.

A family has been brewing and selling spirits without a license. The council cannot agree on whether to fine them or formalize their operation with a license. Meanwhile, the family continues to sell and the licensed tavern owner grows more resentful each week.

The children have organized a trading game with stones and carved tokens. It has its own economy, its own disputes, and its own informal court. The adults find it amusing until they recognize the dynamics as identical to their own.

A donation of building materials from a wealthy family came with the condition that the family's name be displayed prominently on the new structure. The council agreed. Other residents feel the public building now looks like a private monument.

The lamplighter quit because the council reduced his pay. The streets are now dark after sunset and there have been two injuries from falls. The council is looking for a replacement at the reduced rate and finding no takers.

A public notice board was erected near the well. Within a week it was covered in personal grievances, insults, and one surprisingly well-argued policy proposal. The council removed everything and now monitors the board, which rather defeats its purpose.

The founding families hold an annual private dinner to which no one else is invited. It is their right, but the timing: the night before the public harvest feast: feels deliberate. It is a reminder of hierarchy dressed as tradition.

The fence around the communal garden was torn down by someone who believes the garden should be open access. The gardeners who maintain it rebuilt the fence. It was torn down again. The cycle has repeated three times.

A family from a distant settlement arrived requesting permanent residency. The council debated for a week because there is no formal immigration process. In the end they were admitted, but the lack of a clear policy made everyone uncomfortable.

The roofing crew walks off any job that does not include a meal at midday. The tradition started with the original foreman and has outlasted him. Some clients resent feeding workers they are already paying. The crew does not negotiate on this point.

A communal cistern was installed at public expense but the only tap is on the side nearest the council hall. Residents on the far side of the settlement say the placement was self-serving. The council says it was practical. Practicality and self-interest look the same from a distance.

The fletcher's apprentice has surpassed the fletcher in skill. Everyone can see it. The fletcher denies it. The apprentice has started taking private commissions, which the guild rules technically forbid until the apprenticeship ends. Nobody wants to enforce a rule that punishes competence.

A well-respected elder publicly questioned the council's spending during a gathering. The council chair responded by questioning the elder's loyalty. The crowd went quiet and nobody has raised the topic since. Silencing dissent is not the same as resolving the concern.

The night watch caught a council member's nephew outside curfew. The nephew was released without penalty. Every other curfew violation this month resulted in a fine. The inconsistency undermines the watch's authority and everyone involved knows it.

A volunteer crew cleared deadfall from the common wood and stacked it as firewood. The council claimed the firewood as communal property and distributed it according to their own schedule. The volunteers, who did the labor, received no priority. They will not volunteer again.

The stonecutter offered to repair the public fountain for free if the council would credit her workshop publicly. The council refused the credit and asked her to do the work anyway as a civic duty. The fountain remains broken.

A traveling judge visited and settled four pending cases in a single afternoon. The efficiency embarrassed the council, which had been deliberating on the same cases for months. The judge charged a fee that the council calls excessive. The residents who received rulings call it reasonable.

The grain weigher was found using a heavier counterweight for purchases and a lighter one for sales. The discrepancy was small: less than a handful per sack. Over a season, the cumulative gain was substantial. The council fined him and kept him in the role because training a replacement takes months.

A mother petitioned the council to establish a children's play area on the vacant lot near the well. The council approved the plan but allocated no funds. The lot remains vacant and the approval exists only on paper.

The night watch reports are filed weekly but nobody reads them. The watch captain submitted an increasingly absurd entry as a test: he described a dragon landing on the council roof. It was filed without comment. He now questions the entire reporting system.

A household dispute over a shared wall has been escalated to the council four times. Each time the council rules, the losing party appeals. The appeals process has no endpoint and the council has no mechanism to enforce a final decision. The wall, meanwhile, is crumbling.

The settlement's only surviving copy of the founding charter has a water stain across the section that details land rights. The legible portions are invoked constantly. The illegible section is interpreted differently by everyone who claims to remember what it said.

A group of residents organized a work crew to improve the common road without council approval. The road is now better maintained than any council project. The council is considering formalizing the arrangement, which would mean taxing it. The crew says if taxed they will stop.

The apothecary treats ailments for free on the seventh day. The healer considers this an infringement on her practice. The apothecary says healing should not be gated by profession. The patients do not care about the jurisdictional argument. They care about getting well.

A family erected a statue of their ancestor in the public square without approval. The council wants it removed. The family argues it improves the square. The statue is well-crafted and arguably does improve the square, which makes the council's position harder to defend.

The carpenter was hired by the council to build new shelving for the records hall. He submitted an invoice that the council considers inflated. He says the wood was premium grade. The council says they specified standard grade. The written order does not specify grade at all.

A merchant has been paying a premium for exclusive access to the morning market. Other merchants arrive to find the best locations already occupied by her goods. Technically she is not breaking any rule. The other merchants are now demanding a rule.

The barber doubles as the tooth-puller and charges the same rate for both. A haircut and an extraction cost the same coin. He says the skill required is comparable. The customers who need extractions are not in a position to negotiate.

The beekeeper's hives are positioned upwind of the schoolhouse. The children are stung regularly. The beekeeper was asked to move the hives and refused on the grounds that the bees were there first. The school was built three years after the hives.

A census was taken and the results were disputed by four households who claim the count missed members who were traveling. The council says the census counts who is present, not who belongs. The distinction matters because resource allocation follows the count.

An elderly resident wanders the settlement after dark and has been mistaken twice for an intruder by the watch. The family was asked to keep him indoors. They say they cannot. The watch was asked to recognize him. They say they cannot guarantee it in the dark.

The communal herb garden was divided into private plots by a council decision that was made while three dissenting members were absent. The absent members say the vote was illegitimate. The present members say absence is not dissent.

A skilled carpenter arrived from another settlement offering to work at half the local rate. The guild blocked him from taking commissions. He is working informally despite the guild's objections. The customers are choosing price over protocol.

The council allocated emergency funds for flood repairs after the spring melt. The funds were distributed unevenly: the council members' properties were repaired first. They say the prioritization was based on severity. The damage assessments were conducted by the council themselves.

A common well serves both the upper and lower quarters. The upper quarter recently installed a pump that draws water faster than the natural flow replaces it. By afternoon, the lower quarter finds the well diminished. The upper quarter says they invested in the pump and have a right to use it.

The night soil collection schedule was changed without public notice. Three households missed the new schedule and were fined for overflowing waste. They argue the fines are invalid because notification is required for schedule changes. The council says the notice was posted. It was posted inside the council hall.

A family of weavers proposed a cooperative model where several households share a single loom and split the output. The guild opposes it on the grounds that it devalues individual craft. The weavers say the guild's real concern is control.

The farrier set up shop without joining the guild and has been undercutting the established smith on horseshoe work. The guild wants him expelled. The farmers want their horses shod cheaply. The farrier says he will join the guild when the guild offers him something worth joining for.

A mother of twins has petitioned for double the child allocation from the communal stores. The council says each child receives one share regardless of how many a household has. The mother says one household feeding seven cannot survive on five shares. The math supports her.

The drainage ditch was supposed to be dug last spring. It is now late autumn and the ditch exists only as a line on the council's project map. The lower quarter flooded twice during the summer rains. The council blames labor shortages. The labor crew blames late payment.

A retired schoolmaster has been holding informal lessons at the tavern for anyone who wants to attend. The council views this as an unauthorized school. The attendees view it as a conversation. The distinction apparently matters enough to warrant a formal complaint.

The cooper raised his barrel prices after the oak supply ran short. The brewer, the vintner, and the pickle-maker all complained to the council. The council investigated and concluded the price increase was justified. The customers accepted the finding but switched to cheaper casks from outside the settlement. The cooper is now losing business for being honest about costs.

A young man was caught stealing firewood from the communal pile and claimed he could not afford to buy any. The council fined him the cost of the wood plus a penalty. His family, which was already unable to afford wood, now owes more than before. The system addressed the theft and worsened the problem.

The glassmaker offered to install windows in the schoolhouse at cost. The council delayed approval for so long that the glassmaker sold the glass elsewhere. The schoolhouse still has shutters instead of windows. The council says the timing was unfortunate.

A well-liked guard was transferred to the night shift after disagreeing with the captain publicly. The captain says the transfer was routine. The guard says routine transfers do not happen the day after a public argument.

The weaver and the dyer share a building but not a schedule. The weaver works mornings and the dyer works afternoons. The dye fumes permeate the fabric if the dyer starts early. The dyer started early twice last week and the weaver's output was ruined. Neither will pay for the lost goods.

A group of residents pooled funds to hire a private well-digger after the council declined to dig a second well. The well produces excellent water. The council now wants to designate it as a public resource. The residents who paid for it consider that theft.

The leather-worker's drainage has been running into the neighboring garden for months. The gardener has complained to the council, to the guild, and to the leather-worker directly. The leather-worker installed a diversion that channels the waste to the road instead. The road crew is now involved.

The surveyor was asked to resolve the boundary dispute between two farms and produced a map that satisfied neither party. Both accuse him of bias toward the other. The surveyor says the ground does not care about feelings and the boundary is where it is.

A resident who volunteered for the fire watch was injured while responding to a blaze. The council says volunteers assume their own risk. The volunteer says the council should have provided equipment. The next fire will be answered by fewer volunteers.

A family's goats escaped and ate the schoolmaster's herb garden. The schoolmaster demands compensation. The family offers to replace the herbs. The schoolmaster says some of those plants took years to establish and cannot be replaced with seedlings. He has a point the family does not want to acknowledge.

The tavern keeper proposed hosting council meetings in his establishment and waiving room fees. The council declined, stating it would create a conflict of interest. The tavern keeper noted that the council already holds unofficial meetings there every evening and nobody seems concerned about that conflict.

The midwife delivered three babies in the same week and asked the council for an assistant. The council approved the request in principle but has not funded it. The midwife is still working alone and the next due date is a month away.

A retired soldier moved in last season and offered to train the watch in close-quarters combat. The watch captain refused on the grounds that his methods were outdated. The recruits who attended informal sessions with the soldier disagree.

The ditch digger found a cache of old coins while excavating the drainage line. The council claimed them as communal property. The digger says finders' rights apply. The coins sit in the records hall while the debate continues and the drainage line remains unfinished.

A section of the common fence was knocked down by a loaded cart and nobody has claimed responsibility. Three cart drivers use that road and all deny involvement. The fence remains down and the livestock have noticed.

The lamplighter's apprentice is better at the job than the lamplighter. The lamplighter knows this and has started assigning himself the easier routes while the apprentice handles the steep streets and the far corners. The apprentice has not complained yet, but the imbalance is visible to everyone.

A resident erected a windbreak on her property that blocks the afternoon sun from her neighbor's garden. She is within her rights because the structure is on her land. The neighbor's tomatoes have not ripened since it went up. Being within your rights and being right are different things.

The well rope was replaced with one that is thinner and cheaper. The water carriers say it cuts their hands. The quartermaster says the budget dictated the choice. The previous rope lasted two years. This one will not last one.

Two apprentices from different workshops were caught brawling in the square over a personal dispute. The guild fined both masters for failing to supervise their apprentices. The masters are now feuding with the guild instead of each other, which solved the apprentice problem but created a larger one.

A proposal to build a communal bathhouse was rejected because the council could not agree on the location. Every proposed site was near someone who objected. The proposal has been tabled three times in two years and the settlement still has no bathhouse.

The brewer and the baker share a yeast culture that has been active for eleven years. When the brewer accidentally killed his portion, the baker shared hers without hesitation. The brewer has not reciprocated with a single free pint and the baker keeps a quiet tally.

The cobbler's workshop floods every spring because the drainage was routed past his door by a council decision made before he moved in. Every year he petitions for a redirect. Every year the council says the redirect would flood someone else. The cobbler has waterproofed his floor and stopped asking.

A family keeps goats that escape at least once a month. The neighbors have complained, the council has issued warnings, and the goats continue to escape. The fence has been repaired seven times. At this point the goats are better known than the family.

The council hired a scribe from outside the settlement because no resident met the qualifications. The locals resent an outsider recording their business. The scribe does excellent work and keeps her opinions to herself, which makes her more effective than her predecessor and less trusted.

A public drinking fountain was installed near the market at community expense. Within a month, a merchant moved his stall to block access and redirect foot traffic past his goods. The council asked him to move. He cited his stall permit, which predates the fountain. The fountain is accessible only from one side now.

The night watch caught someone dumping kitchen waste in the alley behind the bakery. The bakery owner demanded the council identify the dumper. The council said they could not compel the watch to reveal the identity without a formal hearing. The alley still smells and the waste keeps appearing.

The farrier charges double on rest days and the council says he has the right to set his own prices. The farmers whose horses throw shoes on rest days disagree, especially since the farrier is the only one within a day's travel.

A charitable collection for a family that lost their home to fire raised three times the rebuilding cost. The excess was held by the council for future emergencies. The donors expected the surplus to go to the family. The family expected it too. The council kept it.

The road crew foreman assigns his son the lightest duties and the longest breaks. The other workers have stopped raising it because the foreman controls shift assignments and those who complain find themselves on the dawn crew for a month.

A merchant donated a clock to the council hall and it has become the settlement's official timekeeper. The clock runs two minutes fast and the merchant knows it. The council schedules around the clock and the merchant always arrives early, which gives him first position at every public meeting.

The woodcutter's guild requires three years of apprenticeship before a member can fell timber independently. A self-taught cutter who has been working for five years was denied membership because he did not serve an apprenticeship. His trees fall straighter than most guild members' but the rules do not measure trees.

A woman who runs the only laundry service raised her rates after the council taxed her water usage. Her clients are angry at her. She is angry at the council. The council is spending the tax revenue on a project that benefits the upper quarter exclusively.

The old meeting hall bench has a carved message on its underside that reads 'the council forgets but the bench remembers.' Nobody knows who carved it or when. The bench has been in continuous use for longer than anyone on the current council has served.

The surveyor's assistant was dismissed after disagreeing publicly with a boundary ruling that favored a council member. The official reason was insubordination. The assistant's measurements were accurate and the ruling stands in contradiction to them.

A widow who brews medicinal tea from her garden has been told she needs a merchant's license to sell from her home. She has been giving the tea away for years and only recently started accepting payment. The distinction between gift and sale is apparently a council concern.

The tailor and the seamstress operate on opposite sides of the market and have an unspoken agreement not to undercut each other. A new arrival who opened a clothing repair stall between them is charging less than both and neither the tailor nor the seamstress will acknowledge the threat publicly. Privately, they are furious.

A dead tree in the square was supposed to be removed last autumn. The council commissioned the work, the woodcutter felled it, and the trunk has been lying across the path for four months because nobody was contracted to haul it away. The commission specified removal. It did not specify what to do with the result.

The council approved a new signpost for the crossroads but the carpenter and the sign painter cannot agree on dimensions. The carpenter built the post to standard size. The painter says the letters will not be legible at that width. The crossroads has an empty post standing in it while two professionals argue about a sign.
